Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I was going to sit down and focus, but then I'd get distracted or overwhelmed by everything else going on. I work part-time and have other commitments, so I'd often end up cramming late at night or squeezing in study sessions whenever I could. I tried to listen to lecture recordings during my commute since I learn better by hearing things, but it wasn't exactly structured. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: It definitely varied a lot based on deadlines and whatever else was happening in my life. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, then basically go quiet for a while. Regular schedules just didn't stick for me this semester. My anxiety would spike when deadlines approached, and that's when I'd suddenly become super active, but it wasn't sustainable or healthy.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or pure panic that got me logging in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed something important, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. The discussion forums also pulled me in occasionally because I actually liked seeing what other students were saying. But if nothing urgent was happening, I could go days without thinking about the LMS.
Q4: For a brief period in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking in most days, especially for the discussion posts. It became almost automatic to open it while having my morning coffee. But then I had a really stressful week with work and personal stuff, and that routine completely fell apart. Once I broke the habit, it was hard to get back into it, and I never really did.
Q5: The recorded lectures were honestly my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I could replay sections I didn't understand and listen while doing other things. The discussion forums were also surprisingly helpful because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click for me. When other students explained things in their own words, it sometimes made more sense than the official materials.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit there. Reading dense text doesn't work well for my brain—I zone out and have to reread the same paragraph five times. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice quizzes because by the time I remembered they existed, I was already behind on required stuff.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to an assignment or exam, I'd actually do it. Also, anything that involved listening or discussion felt worth my time because that's how I actually absorb information. But if something felt like busy work or I couldn't see the point, my motivation just evaporated. With my anxiety levels, I had to be strategic about where I put my limited mental energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're already struggling to keep up with required work and dealing with stress from multiple directions, optional stuff just falls off the radar. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which, as unhealthy as it sounds, was sometimes the only thing that got me moving. Optional things needed to feel genuinely interesting or useful for me to bother.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I downloaded a ton of lecture recordings and listened to them offline, on the bus, while cooking, wherever. I also had a study group chat with a few classmates where we'd share notes and explain things to each other. I used YouTube videos to understand concepts the lectures didn't make clear. A lot of my actual learning happened outside the LMS, just using what I'd pulled from it.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to a downloaded lecture while walking around or doing chores—definitely learning, but the LMS would have no idea. Or I'd spend an hour discussing course concepts with my study group over coffee. I also did a lot of thinking and processing while doing mindfulness exercises, which sounds weird, but sometimes concepts would suddenly make sense when my brain was calm.
Q11: Yeah, constantly. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it downloaded correctly, then never touch the LMS version again. Same with PDFs—download once, study from my laptop's folder. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so offline access was essential. The LMS probably thought I watched things once and never came back, but I was actually reviewing them multiple times.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed. My anxiety was through the roof, I was exhausted from work, and I started feeling like I was too far behind to catch up. That feeling of being overwhelmed made it so hard to even open the LMS because I knew I'd just see all the things I hadn't done. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit with the anxiety, but it didn't fix the underlying problem of being behind.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed, I'd log in, stare at the screen, and accomplish nothing. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than ask for help, which obviously made things worse. Work deadlines competed directly with school deadlines, and work usually won because I needed the paycheck. My LMS usage got really erratic during those times.
Q14: So many times. I'd have a video playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open materials and just feel this wall of anxiety that prevented me from actually absorbing anything. My body was there, my brain was not.
Q15: The exam prep was the big one I kept putting off. I knew it was crucial but I was so anxious about how far behind I was that I just... couldn't face it. It's like this paradox where the more important something is, the more terrifying it becomes when you're already struggling. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own problems.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, which is not great. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, then crash afterward. The LMS would probably show these spikes of engagement followed by silence. It wasn't healthy and it definitely affected the quality of my work, but it was the only way I could function given everything else going on.
Q17: When I got feedback, it was kind of a mixed bag. Good feedback motivated me for like a day, but then the feeling would fade. Critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid that assignment or topic afterward. I wish I'd been better at using feedback constructively, but when you're already stressed, it's hard not to take criticism personally.
Q18: The weekly structure was actually helpful when I could keep up with it, but once I fell behind, it became overwhelming. Having so many online courses at once meant I was constantly juggling different schedules and deadlines. The discussion requirements kept me somewhat connected to the course even when I was struggling with other components. But the flexibility that online learning offers was also a trap for someone like me who struggles with self-discipline.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were also engaging because they felt more interactive. But the heavy reading requirements were really hard for me. Also, having assignments worth a lot of points but with unclear instructions spiked my anxiety and made me avoid them until the last minute.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd be in the LMS a lot, watching videos, reading discussions, even participating in conversations, but when it came to actually submitting assignments, I'd freeze up. The anxiety of being judged on my work was different from just passively consuming content. Also, understanding something and being able to produce work demonstrating that understanding are two very different things.
Q21: I can see that happening if someone is efficient or learns well from external resources. Some people can read a textbook once and get it. That's not me at all—I need repetition and audio input. But I imagine someone with better study skills and less anxiety could probably do well without logging in much. The LMS activity doesn't equal learning for everyone.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you engage with different materials. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually focused or just going through the motions. They can't see offline studying, group discussions, or the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They definitely can't measure anxiety or how hard you're actually trying despite struggling.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just about clicking through materials or showing up—it's about being present and curious. I felt engaged during discussions and when listening to lectures that interested me, even if my grades don't reflect that. Engagement is internal, and sometimes it doesn't translate to external performance.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was actually trying despite my anxiety holding me back. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or discussing concepts with classmates. It wouldn't capture the mindfulness sessions I did trying to manage my stress enough to study. And it definitely wouldn't show how surprised and disappointed I am that despite all that effort, I still failed. The data tells one story, but it's not the whole story.
